title: Murmurations of Arithmetic L-functions
speaker: Andrew Sutherland
abstract: While conducting a series of number-theoretic machine learning experiments, He, Lee, Oliver, and Pozdnyakov noticed a curious oscillation in the averages of Frobenius traces of elliptic curves over Q.  If one computes the average value of a_p(E) for E/Q of fixed rank with conductor in a given range, as p increases the average of a_p(E) oscillates with a decaying frequency determined by the conductor.  The shape of these oscillations depends on the parity and magnitude of the  rank.  That the rank influences the distribution of Frobenius traces haslong been known (indeed,

proof_stages.label: Sato-Tate baseline for Frobenius traces
proof_stages.summary: The talk first fixes the large-scale expectation: normalized Frobenius traces of elliptic curves and abelian varieties are governed by compact Sato-Tate groups, whose identity components and component groups already affect trace distributions.

proof_stages.label: Rank bias, Nagao sums, and conductor scale
proof_stages.summary: The Birch-Swinnerton-Dyer perspective explains a secondary finite-prime bias in traces through rank, while Nagao-type sums and effective multiplicity-one considerations indicate that the conductor controls the scale on which such information becomes visible.

proof_stages.label: Elliptic-curve murmurations by conductor
proof_stages.summary: The original machine-learning observation is reformulated as conductor-window averages of Frobenius traces separated by rank or root number, and larger LMFDB-scale computations show stable oscillatory crossover patterns rather than mere rank bias.

proof_stages.label: Conductor ordering and arithmetic L-function normalization
proof_stages.summary: Alternative orderings by naive height, discriminant, or j-invariant fail to reveal the same murmurations, motivating a move to arithmetic L-functions with motivic-weight normalization of coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Modular-form generalization
proof_stages.summary: Weight-2, weight-4, and weight-6 newform data, with Galois orbits and the appropriate p-normalization, show analogous murmuration curves whose shapes depend on the weight.

proof_stages.label: Abelian-surface and Sato-Tate-group variation
proof_stages.summary: The experiments are extended to genus-2 Jacobians and abelian surfaces, comparing generic USp(4), imprimitive SU(2) x SU(2), and its normalizer to test how the Sato-Tate group and primitivity affect murmurations.

proof_stages.label: Local averaging in the p/conductor variable
proof_stages.summary: A new local-averaging method bins each L-function’s coefficients according to the ratio p/N, reducing smearing from wide conductor intervals and sharply exposing curves across elliptic curves, modular forms, and genus-2 data.

proof_stages.label: Thin families and rescaled x-axis
proof_stages.summary: The closing addenda test local averaging on a thin quadratic-twist family and suggest, in light of Nina’s theorem, that a square-root rescaling of the p/N axis may be closer to the natural scale.

examples.name: Rank-28 elliptic curve Sato-Tate animation
examples.note: A high-rank elliptic curve is used to make concrete that strong finite-prime bias in Frobenius traces is nevertheless forced into the Sato-Tate semicircle in the limit.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the rank-28 elliptic-curve animation by sampling more Frobenius traces, computing the coefficients for primes up to growing bounds, and interpreting the limiting Sato-Tate semicircle despite the rank bias

examples.name: CM elliptic curve spike distribution
examples.note: CM elliptic curves over the ground field and over Q illustrate how different Sato-Tate groups, especially disconnected normalizers, change the trace histogram.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes the CM trace distribution and its middle spike as a known Sato-Tate-group phenomenon, but does not compute or check it on a specific CM curve

passes.reason: A CM elliptic curve has maximal endomorphism symmetry and its Sato–Tate trace law has a collapsed atom at trace zero.

A CM elliptic curve has extra endomorphism symmetry producing the non-generic spike distribution, but it is presented as a standard case rather than a refutation.

examples.note: A specific genus-2 curve is used to show the higher-dimensional analogue of Sato-Tate trace equidistribution and to emphasize how little of it is currently provable.

examples.name: Nagao-sum charts for ranks 0 through 28
examples.note: Smallest-conductor elliptic curves of ranks 0 to 28, followed by a CM comparison chart, illustrate how Nagao-type sums track rank well at small conductor but converge slowly for high-rank/high-conductor curves.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes and plots Nagao-type sums up to 10^12 for elliptic curves of ranks 0 through 28 and analyzes how the curves approach the predicted rank-dependent limits

passes.reason: The charts use smallest-conductor representatives across the extreme displayed rank range 0 through 28.
The Nagao-sum charts use curves chosen with smallest known conductor for each rank up to the high-rank endpoint 28, and serve to illustrate finite convergence behavior.

The charts use smallest-conductor examples across ranks and include the extreme high-rank 28 case, serving to illustrate Nagao-sum behavior.
The Nagao-sum plots use a curated range reaching the very high-rank endpoint 28, with small-conductor representatives, to show rank-dependent bias.
The Nagao-sum plots use curves selected across the extreme rank range up to rank 28 and with small/minimal conductors for those ranks, as evidence rather than refutation.
The Nagao-sum charts use curves chosen at the smallest known conductors for ranks up to the extreme high rank 28, as an illustration of rank-sensitive convergence.
The Nagao-sum charts are built from a deliberately extreme rank range up to 28 with small-conductor representatives, to illustrate rank-detection behavior.
The charts use curves chosen at the rank/conductor extremes, spanning ranks up to the high-rank endpoint 28.

examples.name: Quadratic twists of 11a1
examples.note: The thin family of quadratic twists of a fixed elliptic curve demonstrates that local averaging can reveal oscillations even where dyadic conductor windows would be computationally ineffective.
examples.engagement_reason: he fixes 11a1, applies the local-averaging murmuration construction to its quadratic twists, and interprets the resulting oscillations and growth of conductor/height in that thin family

passes.reason: Quadratic twists of 11a1 form a standard infinite twist family used to test whether murmurations persist in a thin family.
The quadratic-twist family is a thin fixed-geometric-isomorphism family based on the minimal-conductor curve 11a1, used to show murmurations persist in such a family.
